Nothing especially fashionable, but with the kind of accidental style that comes from being more concerned with comfort and concealment than presentation. ## Motives, allegiances, relationships Eve’s immediate motive is to work out whether the midnight text is a threat, a prank, or a sign that something is genuinely wrong. Underneath that, though, she’s motivated by a deeper discomfort with being lied to. She hates the idea that everyone else knows the story and she’s being left out of it. Her strongest loyalty is to her best friend, Mina, even though they rub each other the wrong way constantly. Eve also feels an almost inconvenient loyalty to her mum, who is stressed and defensive but clearly trying. Her suspicion is aimed mostly at adults who dodge things and at the people in her orbit who say “it’s nothing” when it obviously isn’t. She is cautiously close to her younger brother, Ben, who she acts as though is annoying but actually watches over more than she admits. She has a difficult relationship with her dad, who is emotionally absent even when physically present. Her attitude toward him has become a kind of trained indifference that is probably more effortful than actual anger. ## Flavour dialogue / internal monologue - “Brilliant. Blood emojis. Very normal. Love that for me.” - “If this is a prank, I’ll actually be furious, which is embarrassing because it means I care.” - “I wasn’t being dramatic. I was being correct, which is much less fun for everyone else.” --- # Character 2: Mina Iqbal ## Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws Mina is Eve’s best friend and the sort of person who can turn a dead Wednesday into something almost survivable. She is funny in a quicker, more verbal way than Eve, and less embarrassed by her own feelings. She is the one who says the thing everybody is thinking but no one wants to admit. That makes her both invaluable and occasionally infuriating. She comes from a big, loud family and has learned to compete for space. As a result, she talks fast, interrupts cheerfully, and can escalate a minor observation into a full theory within seconds. She is not naturally subtle. She is also not easily frightened, which means she can sometimes misread other people’s fear as overreaction. In this story, that could make her dismissive early on, then fiercely useful once she realises the text matters. A few grounding quirks: - She always has a pack of mints, half a packet of biscuits, or a stray charger in her bag. - She quotes things wrong on purpose because she thinks it’s funnier. - She names people’s moods with absolute confidence, even when wrong. - She can turn any situation into a group chat thread. - She hates silence and will fill it with gossip, bad impressions, or a story about something humiliating that happened to her cousin. Her flaw is that she can be a bit too eager to make a mess into a joke. That’s a defence mechanism, but it can also mean she misses when something is truly serious. She can be loyal and reckless in equal measure. ## Physicality Mina is compact, energetic, always moving. She gestures a lot, leans in too close when she talks, and sits as if she’s about to stand up again immediately. Her expressions are rapid-fire: disbelief, amusement, suspicion, and affection all flick across her face very quickly. She wears her school uniform in a way that suggests she is actively annoyed by it. Her hair is usually either perfectly managed or obviously in rebellion. She has the physical confidence of someone who doesn’t mind being seen. Where Eve folds inward, Mina expands outward. ## Motives, allegiances, relationships Mina’s main allegiance is to Eve, but she also has a strong instinct to protect the group’s social stability. She dislikes being left out of secrets, but she dislikes unnecessary panic even more. Early on, she wants to help Eve mostly by translating the problem into something manageable: a prank, an ex, a weird coincidence. She has a warm, complicated relationship with her own family and a low tolerance for anyone acting like “family” automatically means safe. She is likely to be the first person Eve tells, and the first person who can push Eve to stop overthinking and actually act. Her loyalty is real, but not always tactful. ## Flavour dialogue / internal monologue - “Okay, but if someone sends three blood drops at midnight, that’s either pathetic or extremely unserious murder behaviour.” - “You cannot just sit there with your eyes doing that and call it calm.” - “I’m not saying it’s definitely bad. I’m saying it’s bad enough to be weird, which is worse in our town.” --- # Character 3: Ben Mercer ## Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws Ben is Eve’s younger brother, probably fourteen or fifteen, and the kind of boy who acts as though he’s too cool to be observed while simultaneously wanting to be noticed on his own terms. He is at that stage where he thinks he knows everything but still needs food brought upstairs if he’s upset. He has the family’s tendency toward emotional deflection, but it comes out differently from Eve’s. Where she gets sharp, he gets performative: eye-rolling, smirking, dramatic sighs, exaggerated indifference. He is not as oblivious as he looks. He notices more than adults give him credit for, but he often uses knowingness to avoid being vulnerable. A few grounding quirks: - He watches videos with the volume too low and subtitles on, then denies it. - He steals crisps from the kitchen and claims they were “just there.” - He says “calm” in a tone that means the opposite. - He keeps things in his room in highly specific places and gets irrationally defensive if someone moves them. - He knows more about everyone’s business than he should, but often only in fragments. His flaw is that he doesn’t always understand the cost of information. He may blurt out a half-truth at exactly the wrong time, or hide something because he thinks it will make life easier. ## Physicality Ben is thinner than he thinks he is, with a permanently slouched posture that’s trying to look casual and landing somewhere near defensive. He wears hoodies constantly and looks as if he has either just woken up or is offended by the concept of being awake. He has the slightly pinched expression of someone who is trying very hard not to be laughed at. ## Motives, allegiances, relationships Ben’s allegiance is mostly to himself, then to Eve when it really matters, then to whoever is easiest to avoid conflict with. He likes his sister more than he admits and is secretly relieved when she pays attention to him, even if she does it in a bossy way. He is also close enough to the family’s adult secrets to have picked up things he doesn’t fully understand. In the plot, Ben can function as a small but important source of accidental truth. He might reveal a location, a remembered detail, or a family conversation he was not supposed to hear. ## Flavour dialogue / internal monologue - “Why are you looking at me like that? I literally just came in.” - “It’s not my fault everyone in this house talks like it’s a hostage situation.” - “I didn’t say it was important. I said I remembered it.” --- # Character 4: Tamsin Mercer ## Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws Tamsin is Eve and Ben’s mother. She is in permanent managerial mode: working, sorting, remembering, resenting, and trying not to let the house fall apart. She is not cruel, but she is tired in a way that can look like sharpness. She has learned to keep going by treating emotions as something to be dealt with later, which of course means they are mostly being stored in the walls. She is the kind of parent who says “we’ll talk later” and genuinely means it, except later never comes because there is laundry, bills, a phone call, and work. She loves her children but is not especially good at being legible to them. Her flaw is that she assumes managing the practical side of life is close enough to caring for the emotional side. A few grounding quirks: - She leaves half-finished mugs of tea in every room. - She talks to herself while doing chores, usually about lists. - She apologises for things while still doing them. - She has a drawer for everything and yet can never find the thing she needs. - She becomes more evasive the more frightened she is. Her secret burden in the story is that she has helped keep something quiet, probably because she thought silence was safer than the truth. ## Physicality Tamsin moves with efficient fatigue. She does everything slightly too quickly, as though pausing might make the whole day collapse. Her hair is usually clipped up badly. She has the hands of someone who is always washing, carrying, sorting, folding. Her face is capable of tenderness, but more often it is set into a practical expression that makes even kindness sound like a task. ## Motives, allegiances, relationships Her main allegiance is to her children, but she has also been trying to protect the family’s fragile stability and herself from having to confront an old event. She may be loyal to another adult involved in the accident — a sibling, partner, or friend — which complicates her choices. She is not acting out of villainy; she is acting out of fear, shame, and the exhausting desire to keep life from getting worse. She and Eve love each other deeply and irritate each other constantly. Their conflict comes from how similar they are: both can be sharp, both can go quiet, both hate being cornered. ## Flavour dialogue / internal monologue - “Not now, love. I’m dealing with something.” - “I know you want answers. I’m just not doing this at midnight.” - “You think I’m hiding things because I enjoy it?” --- # Character 5: Gareth Mercer ## Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws Gareth is Eve’s father, though “father” is doing more work than “present.” He is intermittently around, often in a way that makes everyone tense. He can be superficially easygoing, the kind of man who cracks jokes when things get awkward, but there is something evasive in him. He prefers to smooth things over rather than explain them, and he assumes that not saying something is the same as protecting people. He may have been involved in the old accident or cover-up, directly or indirectly. If so, his guilt is the defensive sort: not melodramatic, but stubborn and self-justifying. He is the sort of adult who says he didn’t want to burden anyone, as though burdening everyone else with confusion was better. A few grounding quirks: - He overexplains simple things and underexplains important ones. - He always seems to be half-listening to something else. - He uses humour as a smoke screen. - He touches the back of his neck when cornered. - He is the kind of man who will say “it was a long time ago” about something that changed somebody’s life. His flaw is avoidance. He likely believes he is keeping the peace, but really he is preserving his own discomfort. ## Physicality Gareth has the slack physical confidence of someone who still thinks he’s easier to read than he is. He’s not especially neat, not especially imposing, but he occupies space as if he expects to be forgiven for it. He smiles too quickly when challenged and looks away too often when things get serious. ## Motives, allegiances, relationships His allegiance is to avoiding collapse: of the family, of his own image, of the old story. If he did help cover up the accident, he may tell himself he did it for love. He is emotionally distant from Eve, though not entirely indifferent. The relationship is painful because he still wants to be seen as decent, even when he hasn’t earned that feeling. ## Flavour dialogue / internal monologue - “It wasn’t like that, was it?” - “You’re making a big thing out of nothing.” - “I was trying to protect you. That counts for something.” --- # Character 6: Imogen Vale ## Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws Imogen is the person who sends the text, or is closest to doing so — depending on the exact shape of the reveal. She is likely a slightly older teenager, or a young adult connected to the family or the old accident. She has the hard, compressed energy of someone who has been carrying a secret badly for too long. If she has been quiet, it is not because she is empty; it is because she has been trying not to spill. She is not polished or obviously villainous. Her message arrives after midnight because that’s when fear won. She is the kind of person who sends a dramatic text, deletes three follow-ups, then stares at the screen feeling stupid and panicked. Her flaw is that she might be impulsive at exactly the wrong moment and then morally rigid about it afterwards. A few grounding quirks: - She writes messages and deletes them before sending, then sends the ugliest version by mistake. - She overuses punctuation when stressed. - She is very aware of other people’s lies. - She can seem cold because she has decided sentimentality is dangerous. - She remembers details no one else thought mattered. ## Physicality Imogen is lean, watchful, and a bit overcontrolled physically, as if she is always trying not to reveal how rattled she is. She stands with her weight shifted back, not quite relaxed, and tends to look at people from under her eyelashes, measuring what they’ll admit. Her appearance is not flashy, but there is something tense in the way she carries herself. ## Motives, allegiances, relationships Her motive is to force the truth into daylight, but she doesn’t do it elegantly. She may be loyal to the person who was injured, or betrayed by the family’s silence, or desperate to stop Eve from being lied to any longer. She has one foot in the world of the adults’ secret and one in the world of the teenagers who are being kept in the dark. She is useful as a character because she complicates the idea of “sender as villain.” She may have acted selfishly, but her fear is real, and her reasons are human. ## Flavour dialogue / internal monologue - “I didn’t know how else to say it.” - “You all kept acting as if ignoring it made it disappear.” - “You think sending three blood emojis is mad? Fine. It was. I was mad.” --- If you want, I can now turn these into a **relationship map** and a **character-function breakdown by chapter** so we can see exactly who does what in the 8-part structure.

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 1 It came just after midnight. Eve saw the screen light up under the duvet and had the instant, unreasonable certainty that something terrible had happened, which was annoying because the next feeling was irritation. Her phone buzzed again against her thigh, buzzing like it had somewhere important to be. She fumbled it free with one hand, the other still trapped beneath the blanket, and squinted at the message. 🩸🩸🩸 That was it. No text. No name. No number she recognised, either, just the silent little row of red droplets sitting in the dark like they were pleased with themselves. Eve stared at it for a second, then let her head fall back on the pillow. “Oh, for fuck’s sake,” she whispered to nobody. She checked the time. 12:07. Because apparently midnight was now a time for nonsense from the universe. She sat up, hair sticking up at the back, and tapped the profile. Unknown number. No photo. No saved contact. She copied the number into Messages in case she needed to block it later, then immediately did not block it because that would feel like letting it win. Her thumb hovered over the keyboard. Who is this? No. Too sensible. What do you want? Too dramatic. Wrong number? Too hopeful. She stared at the three blood drops until her eyes went slightly strange, as if maybe if she looked long enough they’d turn into something useful. A warning, perhaps. A clue. A joke. A mistake. Her phone buzzed again. Eve nearly threw it. Then saw it was Mina, which somehow made it worse. **Mina:** u awake **Mina:** don’t say yes if ur dead **Mina:** actually if ur dead dont reply but also maybe do??? Eve snorted once, despite herself. **Eve:** i’ve had a text from satan’s wet cousin **Mina:** ??? **Eve:** 🩸🩸🩸 **Mina:** oh that’s deeply unwell **Mina:** who is it from **Eve:** no clue **Mina:** ex? **Eve:** if it was an ex i’d have the dignity of knowing which one **Mina:** fair Eve put the phone on her chest and stared at the ceiling. The fan made a faint ticking sound. Somewhere downstairs the boiler clicked in its sleep. The house had that dead, listening quiet it got at night, as if every room was pretending not to be there. She should have gone back to sleep. That would have been the sensible thing. Instead she kept thinking of the message, and then thinking of why the colour had bothered her so much. Blood was too specific to be funny and too stupid to be threatening, which somehow made it worse. If someone was messing with her, they’d chosen an excellent time. Her brain at midnight was basically wet wallpaper. The number was not saved to anything she knew. She checked the recent calls. Nothing. She scrolled through old group chats, because apparently there was no limit to how embarrassing she was prepared to be before dawn. Year eleven trip. Drama club. Mina’s cousin’s birthday chat. A forgotten thread with Ben in which he’d once sent thirty-seven skull emojis because he was going through a phase and had opinions about trap music. Nothing. Then she paused. Not because of the message. Because of the feeling that came with it. That odd, cold flicker of recognition you got when something reminded you of something else before you knew what. She dragged the blanket up around her knees and tried to think clearly, which was already a mistake. A blood emoji wasn’t random. Not if someone had sent it to her. Not if they knew where to put it. She looked at the number again. Then, after a moment she would later claim she hadn’t meant to have, she opened her saved photos and searched for the old picture. It took three tries. She had renamed it something stupid months ago so she wouldn’t have to look at it too often. There it was: a blurry shot taken in daylight outside the old leisure centre, rain on the pavement, three teenagers half-out of frame and looking furious at each other in the way only teenagers can manage. Eve had cropped herself out of it because she hated how she looked in that coat. Mina was in the middle with her hood up. And next to them, just visible, was Imogen Vale, all sharp elbow and unsmiling mouth, staring straight at the camera like she already knew the whole thing had gone wrong. The photo had been taken the week after the incident. The incident. As if calling it that made it smaller. Eve stared at the screen so long her eyes began to ache. There were three of them in the picture. Three people who’d been there that night. Three people who had said, in different ways, do not talk about this. She had not thought about that photo for months. Not properly. She’d shoved it into a folder and then into a corner of her mind where inconvenient things went to mould. Her stomach gave a small, unpleasant lurch. Then another buzz. For one second she thought the unknown number had texted again, but it was Ben, which was somehow even more irritating because Ben was never awake at this hour unless he was unwell or being a goblin. **Ben:** u awake **Ben:** why is mum in the kitchen crying **Ben:** she said she isnt crying btw **Ben:** lying is weird at 12am Eve sat up so fast the blanket slid off her legs. She stared at the messages. Then at the dark gap beneath her door. Then back at the screen. Another message came through, this time from Mum. **Mum:** Are you awake? Please stay in your room. The sentence was so wrong in its calmness that Eve felt a prickle at the back of her neck. She typed **what happened** and deleted it. Typed **is ben okay** and deleted that too. Then she heard it. Not loud. Nothing dramatic. Just the soft, unmistakable sound of someone moving downstairs very carefully, trying not to wake the house. A cupboard door. A thud, quickly muffled. Then Mum’s voice, thin and tense: “Not here.” Eve was out of bed before she’d decided to be. Her feet hit the cold floorboards. The room was full of shadows and the stale smell of sleep. She crossed to the door, hand on the handle, and stopped. The unknown number still glowed on her screen. 🩸🩸🩸 Under it, in the contact preview, a new message had appeared. Her finger trembled as she unlocked the phone. Just one line. **You need to look at what happened three years ago.** Below that, another message started to come through. Only half of it loaded before the screen dimmed: **Don’t let her—** Eve didn’t breathe. Downstairs, something crashed. Not a huge crash. Not the sort of thing that made the walls shake. Just enough to make her think of glass. Or a dropped tray. Or somebody knocking into the side of the sink too hard. Then her mum said, in a voice that was trying and failing to stay level, “No, Gareth. Don’t you start.” Eve stood frozen in the dark with the phone in her hand and the blood emojis burning a hole in the screen. Three years ago. Three people. Three drops. She had the sudden, sick feeling that she was already behind, and had been for a very long time.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine command of voice, pacing, and tonal balance. Let me work through the key elements critically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Eve's voice is the chapter's greatest asset. The internal monologue feels authentically teenage without being patronising or clichéd. The sequence where she considers what to text back ("Too sensible. Too dramatic. Too hopeful.") is economical and character-revealing. Her self-awareness about her own embarrassing behaviour ("scrolled through old group chats, because apparently there was no limit to how embarrassing she was prepared to be before dawn") lands well. The defensive humour is consistent with the profile and feels earned rather than performed. DIALOGUE: The text exchange with Mina is genuinely good — it captures the rhythm of late-night teenage messaging without trying too hard. "Satan's wet cousin" is a nice coinage. Ben's texts are brief but characterful. The one line of Tamsin's dialogue ("Not here") and the overheard "No, Gareth. Don't you start" do real work efficiently. The dialogue is one of the chapter's strengths. PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The text arrives, Eve investigates, she contacts Mina, she finds the old photo connecting the message to a past incident, and the chapter ends with escalating tension — Ben's message about Tamsin crying, the overheard argument, and the partial second message from the unknown number. The "three" motif is introduced without being hammered. The chapter ending creates genuine forward momentum. METAPHOR AND PROSE: Most of the prose is clean and purposeful. "The house had that dead, listening quiet it got at night, as if every room was pretending not to be there" is a good image that earns its slight poeticism. "Her brain at midnight was basically wet wallpaper" is funny and apt. "The blood emojis burning a hole in the screen" is a minor cliché but not egregious. "Sitting in the dark like they were pleased with themselves" applied to emojis is a nice touch. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly — "the stale smell of sleep" is fine but generic — but nothing that tips into purple territory. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly over-reliant on the rule of three as a structural device (three emojis, three people, three years, three drops) — it's introduced a bit mechanically at the end. The line "Three years ago. Three people. Three drops." as a closing internal monologue is the chapter's weakest moment: it tells us the thematic connection rather than letting us feel it, and it's the one place where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious. The photo discovery is a touch convenient — the protagonist just happens to have a relevant photo saved under a deliberately obscure name — though this is a minor complaint for an opening chapter that needs to establish connections quickly. The "Don't let her—" partial message is a solid cliffhanger device, though it risks feeling slightly thriller-generic. The chapter earns its darkness through accumulation of small, real details rather than melodrama, which is exactly right for the genre. FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter hits all its planned beats: midnight text, annoyance then chill, screenshot and friend contact, the detail that makes it feel personal (the photo of Imogen), and ending with a memory the protagonist would rather not have. The addition of the overheard parental argument and Ben's texts adds urgency that slightly exceeds the plan in a good way. OVERALL: This is confident, well-controlled YA writing with a strong sense of place and voice. The tonal balance between comedy and dread is well-managed. The chapter does what it needs to do without overreaching. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 2 By eight-thirty the next morning, Eve had managed to look mostly normal, which in her family counted as a form of fraud. She’d showered, which made her feel marginally less haunted. She’d put on the school jumper that made her look as though she had no opinions at all, which was useful. She’d eaten half a piece of toast over the sink while trying not to think about the sound of something crashing downstairs at one in the morning. Nobody had explained anything. Her mum had knocked on her door at 7:12 and said, too brightly, that everyone was fine and she’d been “a bit upset” and there was no need for Eve to make it into a thing. Which was, Eve had thought, exactly how people behaved when a thing absolutely existed. Gareth had already left for work, or been asked to leave, or both. Ben had emerged at breakfast with the air of a boy trying to determine whether the household was in crisis or just being weird again. He had failed to get a straight answer and had gone to school muttering that adults were “freaks”. Which, for Ben, counted as an analysis. Eve kept checking her phone under the desk in first period, though nothing had changed except the fact that she now had three unread messages from Mina, one from Ben asking if she had any clue what was going on, and one from an unknown number that she had not opened again because she was not emotionally committed to falling apart before lunch. Mina met her at the lockers with a look that said she had been holding onto her theory all morning and was eager to weaponise it. “You look knackered,” she said. “Thanks.” “No, seriously. You look like someone’s used you as a witness statement.” Eve slammed her locker shut. “I’ve had a weird night.” “Mhm. We all saw that coming.” Eve glanced up and down the corridor. Sixth form was already in motion around them: blazers hanging open, ties half-done, people pretending not to be late, the usual smell of wet coats and cheap coffee and whatever madness lived in the sports hall. A teacher in a navy jumper was trying to look as though he hadn’t noticed two boys wrestling over a charger. “What if,” Mina said, lowering her voice with deliberate theatricality, “it’s an ex.” Eve gave her a look. “What?” Mina said. “It’s always an ex. Or a stalker. Or a dead aunt. But mostly an ex.” “I haven’t had enough interesting relationships to acquire a stalker.” “You say that like it’s a flaw.” Eve showed her the text. Mina’s face changed, just a little. Not alarmed exactly. More like she’d expected a joke and hadn’t got one. “Right,” she said. “Right?” “That’s not just weird. That’s properly weird.” “Thank you. That’s the mood I was aiming for.” Mina handed the phone back. “Who is it from?” “No clue. I checked the number.” “Could be a prank.” “Could be.” Mina looked at her more carefully. “Could be about last night.” Eve made a face. “Nothing happened last night.” Mina gave her the sort of look that meant she’d only accept that if the alternative was worse. “You texted me at twelve-fifteen sounding like you’d seen a rat in a coffin.” “That’s not a thing.” “It is now.” Eve leaned against the lockers. She could feel the tiredness still sitting behind her eyes, a dull pressure, and the familiar urge to act as if it was all a joke until it stopped existing. “It started with that,” she said, tapping the phone. “Then my mum told me not to make a thing of it, which is obviously what people say when there is a thing.” “What thing?” “I don’t know.” Mina made a low sound of frustration. “You’re doing that thing where you say ‘I don’t know’ but you definitely know slightly more than you’re saying.” “I know enough to be unsettled.” “Which is annoyingly vague.” “Welcome to my life.” A bell rang somewhere down the corridor. Someone shouted about a geography folder. The corridor compressed and expanded around them in the usual school way, full of movement but not really going anywhere. “Did your mum say anything else?” Mina asked. Eve hesitated. The temptation was there, immediate and stupid, to keep the night tucked away. But Mina had a look about her now, sharp and stubborn, that said she was going to make this somebody else’s problem whether invited or not. “She said everyone was fine,” Eve said. “Then she looked like she wanted to throw a mug at something.” Mina snorted despite herself. “That doesn’t narrow it down much.” “No.” They moved towards their first lesson. Mr. Weller had them for English Literature, which meant thirty-five minutes of pretending to discuss symbolism while everyone secretly checked their phones. Eve sat by the window and didn’t hear a word of the opening discussion about class and voice because she was watching the path outside the building where the rain had left dark stripes on the pavement. Her phone buzzed again under the desk. She checked it so fast she nearly dropped it. Not the unknown number. Ben. **Ben:** there’s a weird old man outside school asking for u **Ben:** not joking **Ben:** he said ur mum’s name Eve stared at the message. Then again. Mina saw her face and mouthed What? Eve typed **who** and hit send. Ben replied almost instantly. **Ben:** idk **Ben:** grey coat **Ben:** looks like every divorced dad in british tv **Ben:** says he knows you **Ben:** says it’s about “the lake” Eve’s stomach went cold in one clean motion. The lake. Not a lake, really. Just the old reservoir behind the disused footpath on the edge of town, the one parents still called the lake because it sounded less grim than “that place where idiots go when they’re bored.” Three years ago, they’d been there. Three years ago, there had been shouting, and someone had slipped, and someone had screamed. Or maybe that was how Eve remembered it. The memory came at her in fragments: wet stone, Mina’s hand on her sleeve, Imogen saying, “Don’t touch him,” and the awful metallic smell that stuck in her throat for days afterwards. She had never really told the story to anyone, because telling it meant choosing what actually happened. And she had never been sure. Eve looked up from the phone to find Mr. Weller staring at her over the top of his glasses. “Everything all right, Mercer?” “Yeah,” she said automatically. It came out wrong. Too quick. Mina had gone still beside her. Mr. Weller waited. Eve lifted her phone a fraction. “My brother’s being weird.” “That is not news,” Mr. Weller said. A few people laughed. Eve did not. She put the phone down, but not before another message landed. Unknown number. **You know where he is.** The words were so plain that they were somehow worse than the emojis. Eve’s skin prickled. She looked through the classroom window again, over the rain-streaked glass and the grey sports field, and thought she saw someone standing by the main gates in a dark coat, half-turned away. She couldn’t tell if it was the man Ben had described or just another parent waiting in the wrong weather. Her heart did a stupid, fast thud. Mina leaned in, voice low. “What?” Eve swallowed. “I think,” she said, because there was no point pretending now, “someone is waiting outside school asking about the reservoir.” Mina’s expression changed from curiosity to something much more serious. “Reservoir?” she said quietly. Eve nodded. Mina looked at her for a long second, then said, “That’s not a prank.” “No,” Eve said. And because her mouth had clearly decided to betray her before her brain could organise itself, she added, “That’s what I’m worried about.” Mr. Weller was still talking about “modern interpretations of narrative unreliability” as if that wasn’t the most insulting phrase in the world to hear at nine-ten on a Monday. Eve stared at her phone while the room blurred slightly at the edges. She checked the unknown number again. No name. No profile picture. Just the same thread, and now, beneath the new text, a location pin had appeared. Not far. Close enough to make her think of walking there on wet grass in the dark. Close enough to make her think of three people standing at the edge of something they’d all agreed never to mention again. At the bottom of the screen, one final message arrived. **Ask your mother what she said happened.** Eve went very still. Across the room, Mina was watching her with the exact expression of someone who had stopped joking because the joke had become real. Eve put her phone flat on the desk and rested both hands on either side of it, as if she might pin the thing down by force. What she wanted, suddenly and violently, was a normal Monday. A stupid lesson. A pointless test. A bus that smelled of wet upholstery. Anything except the shape of that message and the old memory starting to move in the dark. Instead, she had school, and a strange man outside the gates, and her mother’s half-strangled face at midnight. And the terrible certainty that whatever had happened three years ago, it had not stayed buried.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and the established character profiles. Let me work through its qualities and weaknesses systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Eve's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The defensive humour, the self-awareness about caring too much, the way she processes fear through briskness — all of these match the character profile precisely. Lines like "which in her family counted as a form of fraud" and "Welcome to my life" feel genuinely inhabited rather than performed. Mina functions well as the foil: quicker, more verbal, less embarrassed by her own reactions. The dialogue between them has the right texture — competitive, affectionate, slightly combative — and it moves information without feeling like an information-delivery mechanism. Ben's brief text exchange is pitch-perfect: the fragments, the casual observation ("looks like every divorced dad in british tv"), the accidental revelation. Tamsin's brief appearance — the too-bright knock, the "not making it a thing" — efficiently establishes her evasiveness without overexplaining. PLOT MECHANICS: The chapter follows its plan closely. We get the school setting, Mina's dismissiveness shifting to genuine concern, the mention of the reservoir as the connecting thread, and the clue arriving unexpectedly (the man outside school, the location pin, the final message). The escalation from "weird text" to "someone is outside school asking about the reservoir" to "ask your mother what she said happened" is well-paced. Each beat earns the next. The chapter ends on genuine emotional pressure rather than a cheap cliffhanger. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. It sounds young and British without being a parody of either. "You look like someone's used you as a witness statement" is a good line — slightly odd, slightly funny, entirely in character. The exchange about exes and stalkers has the right rhythm. Mr. Weller's brief intervention is economical and effective. The text messages are particularly well-handled: Ben's voice in text form is distinct from his spoken voice would be, which is a subtle but real achievement. SHOWING VS TELLING: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, but there are moments where it tips toward explanation. "Eve's stomach went cold in one clean motion" is fine, but the paragraph that follows — explaining the reservoir memory in some detail — is slightly more expository than the rest of the chapter's texture. The memory is necessary, but the delivery is a touch mechanical: "wet stone, Mina's hand on her sleeve, Imogen saying..." This reads more like a summary than a recovered fragment. It's not a serious flaw, but it's the chapter's most visible seam. METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is generally clean and controlled. There are a few moments where the writing reaches for something slightly more literary — "the old memory starting to move in the dark" is effective; "the joke had become real" is a bit flat. The description of the school corridor ("full of movement but not really going anywhere") is a nice observation that earns its place. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are no strained metaphors that don't land. TONE: The darkly comic British YA tone is well-maintained. The humour never undercuts the tension; it coexists with it, which is exactly what the plan called for. Mr. Weller's "narrative unreliability" line is a good piece of ironic placement — the chapter is about exactly that, and the joke lands without being smug about it. WEAKNESSES: The reservoir memory paragraph is the main structural weakness — it tells us what happened rather than letting us feel it through Eve's reaction. The final few paragraphs, while effective, lean slightly into the "protagonist cataloguing their own emotional state" mode that can feel a touch self-conscious. "What she wanted, suddenly and violently, was a normal Monday" is a reasonable line but not a surprising one. The chapter's ending is strong but not quite as sharp as it could be — "it had not stayed buried" is a slightly clichéd formulation for an otherwise fresh piece of writing. OVERALL: This is a confident, well-executed chapter that delivers on its plan, maintains consistent characterisation, and advances the plot with genuine momentum. Its weaknesses are minor and mostly confined to a few moments of over-explanation. It reads like the work of a writer who knows what they're doing and is mostly doing it. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 3 By lunch, the story had already begun to rot. Not the real story. That was still hidden away in whatever unpleasant corner of the town it lived in. This was the school version, which meant it had been chopped up, improved, and made stupid almost immediately. Apparently there was a man outside the gates asking after “a girl” and “some water thing.” Apparently Eve had a stalker. Apparently Ben had invented the whole thing because he was bored. Apparently there had been a death. Apparently there had been a body. Apparently everybody had known all along. Eve sat with Mina in the sixth-form common room, trying not to look like she wanted to shake the entire room by the shoulders. “People are saying you’ve got a dead body in your garden,” Mina said, peering over the edge of her packet of crisps. “I don’t have a garden large enough for a dead body.” “Hardly the point.” “I’m serious.” “So am I. You’d need a decent spade.” Eve kicked her under the table. Mina grinned, then sobered a little when she saw Eve’s face. “Okay. Sorry. What’s actually happened?” Eve leaned in, elbows on knees, and told her the bits she knew without saying the bits she felt. A text just after midnight. Three blood emojis. Her mother crying in the kitchen. The words about three years ago. The stranger at the school gates asking for the reservoir. Mina listened without interrupting, which was enough to make Eve uneasy. When she finished, Mina said, “Right.” Eve gave her a suspicious look. “Why do you keep saying that like a doctor about to remove something?” “Because I’m thinking.” “Oh, God.” “It’s never good when you do that.” Mina ignored her. “Three years ago, you, me, Imogen, and—” “Don’t.” Mina stopped. “Fine. You, me, and Imogen were there.” “Not helpful.” “It is if I’m right.” Eve looked away. Through the glass wall of the common room, students drifted between lessons with the half-committed misery of people who would rather be anywhere else. A boy in Year Twelve was trying to eat a pasty and hold a phone and sulk at the same time. A teacher nearby looked one cup of tea away from leaving teaching forever. Mina lowered her voice. “I think the reservoir isn’t the story. I think it’s the thing the story happened around.” Eve didn’t answer. That was the problem. Mina was probably right. She hated when Mina was right in a way that made her feel seen. Eve opened the messages again and reread the newest one. **Ask your mother what she said happened.** “What if she’s lying?” Eve said. “Your mother?” “No. The unknown number.” She frowned. “Or both.” Mina tore open another pack of crisps as if violence might help. “Then someone wants you to know that the version you’ve got is rubbish.” Eve sat back. There was something almost offensive about how simple that sounded. She thought of her mum’s face at midnight. The way she had said stay in your room. The way her voice had gone thin when Gareth’s name came up. The cupboard sound downstairs. The crash. “Maybe it’s about the accident,” Eve said. Mina made a face. “The accident.” “The thing.” “Can you hear yourself?” “No.” “Right. Useful.” Eve had a sudden thought so sharp it made her shoulders tense. She pulled up the photos on her phone and scrolled until she found the one from outside the leisure centre. “Look.” Mina leaned over. There they were again: three teenagers, wet pavement, miserable expressions, the old leisure centre in the background with its peeling blue sign. Eve had taken the picture from too close up, probably because she’d been angry at the time and wanted evidence of something, even if she hadn’t known what. “Imogen was there,” Eve said quietly. “Obviously.” “She was there after, too. And she’s not in any of the messages.” Mina frowned. “Why would she be?” “I don’t know.” But the thought was already growing shape in her head. Imogen Vale had always been a bit separate from the rest of them. Not popular exactly, but not not popular either. One of those people who hovered at the edge of whatever was happening and somehow remembered all of it. She had left town for college or work or both, and nobody had heard much from her since. Not until now, maybe. Unless it had been her all along. Eve scrolled back through the message thread with the unknown number. No name. No photo. Only the location pin and the line about her mother. It felt too deliberate to be random, and yet too clumsy to be clever. Like somebody had been in a rush. Like somebody had sent the text with shaking hands. “Three drops,” Mina muttered, reading over her shoulder. “Three years ago. Three people in the picture. That’s either meaningful or you’re becoming cursed.” “Don’t.” “Sorry.” Eve was about to say something sharp when Ben called from across the room. “Eve.” She looked up. He was standing in the doorway, hair damp from the rain and face set in a way that told her immediately he had been sent rather than had chosen to be there. He held his phone up like evidence. “Your mum wants you after school,” he said. “By ‘wants’ do you mean ‘needs’ or ‘is angry’?” “Both, probably.” Ben glanced at Mina, then back at Eve. “Also, someone’s been asking about you.” Eve’s pulse jumped. “Who?” Ben shrugged with the absolute uselessness only younger brothers could master. “No clue. Said they knew what happened at the lake.” Mina went very still. Eve stared at him. “What did they look like?” “Oldish. Grey coat. Like he’d just stepped out of a wet newspaper. He was waiting by the main road before. Then he asked if you were the girl from three years ago.” The room seemed to tighten around that sentence. Mina looked between them. “Did he say his name?” Ben shook his head. “No. But he knew Mum’s.” Eve’s mouth had gone dry. “Did he say anything else?” Ben hesitated. “He asked if you’d seen Imogen.” Mina and Eve looked at each other. The common room was suddenly too bright, too warm, full of the ordinary noise of people opening packets and complaining about coursework and pretending not to be alive. Eve could hear her own breathing. “Did you tell him anything?” she asked. Ben rolled his eyes. “No. I’m not an idiot.” “Could’ve fooled me.” “Cheers.” He shoved his hands in his pockets. “I said you were in a lesson. Which is true. For now.” He lingered a second longer, expression shifting into something less smug and more uncertain. “Eve,” he said, quieter, “is this about Dad?” That landed badly. She opened her mouth, but nothing useful came out. Ben looked at her face and made a small, impatient noise. “Great. So it is.” “I didn’t say that.” “You didn’t have to.” He left before she could decide whether to follow him or shout at him or laugh, which was irritating because he’d made the room feel less stable just by asking. Mina was watching her carefully now. “What did he mean, Dad?” she said. Eve rubbed her forehead. “Nothing.” “That’s not convincing.” “I know.” Mina leaned back. “You know what this feels like?” Eve already hated the answer. “Like?” “Like someone is trying to drag up the thing everybody agreed not to touch.” Eve stared at the table. Three years ago, the reservoir. The shouting. The crash of water against the bank below. Someone slipping. Someone swearing. Gareth’s voice, louder than she’d ever heard it. Her mother saying, “You’re not telling her that.” And then — nothing clear. Just the hot rush of panic and the knowledge that adults had begun making decisions in whispers. She had never asked properly because every time she’d come close, the room had gone stiff. There was a difference between not knowing and being made not to know. “Do you think Mum knows who sent it?” Eve asked. Mina shrugged. “Probably enough to be scared.” Eve looked back down at her phone and, for the first time all day, opened the unknown number again. There was now a small map pin under the message thread, but no address, just a nearby road she recognised from the edge of town. Not far from the reservoir. She zoomed in. The pin sat near a disused entrance track off the main road, the sort of place nobody used unless they were lost, drunk, or meeting someone they shouldn’t. Beneath the pin, another message appeared. **I’m not lying. Ask her about the car.** Eve’s stomach turned. The car. That was not part of the picture in her head. Not at all. She had remembered shouting, water, a fall, a hand on her sleeve. Not a car. Mina read over her shoulder and swore softly. “Oh, that’s not good.” “No.” Eve thought of her mother crying in the kitchen. Gareth leaving early. The strange man at the school gates. Imogen’s name, missing from the thread and still somehow in the middle of everything. And now a car. Something had shifted. Not enough to reveal itself, but enough to let the shape of it show through. She tucked her phone away as the bell rang for afternoon registration, the noise of it loud and stupid and completely indifferent. For one brief second she considered doing the sensible thing: going home after school, asking her mum directly, and accepting whatever happened next. Then she remembered her mother’s face when she said don’t make a thing of it. She remembered the message. She remembered that three years ago, somebody had decided what she was allowed to know. Eve stood up so fast her chair scraped the floor. Mina looked up. “Where are you going?” Eve shoved her phone into her pocket. “To find out what they’ve been lying about,” she said. And because she could already feel the panic trying to catch up with her, she added, “Before I’m stuck hearing it from every idiot in school first.”
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me work through the key elements critically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Eve's voice is consistently rendered — dry, defensive, observant, and slightly exhausted. The internal rhythm of her narration ("She hated when Mina was right in a way that made her feel seen") is exactly the kind of self-aware but not self-pitying register the plan calls for. Mina functions well as the faster, more verbal counterpart, and Ben's brief appearance is economical and effective — he drops the crucial "Is this about Dad?" and exits before Eve can process it, which is both characteristically teenage and narratively efficient. The characters are consistent with their profiles throughout. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. The exchange about the garden being too small for a dead body, the spade joke, Mina's "Right" being used like a doctor about to remove something — these feel genuinely British and genuinely teenage without being performed or laboured. The rhythm of interruption and deflection is well-handled. Ben's dialogue is particularly good: terse, slightly sulky, but carrying real weight. The line "It's not my fault everyone in this house talks like it's a hostage situation" from the profile doesn't appear, but what does appear is equally in character. PLOT MECHANICS: The chapter does what it's supposed to do — it advances the investigation, introduces the car as a new clue, names Imogen as a suspect, and ends with Eve deciding to act. The "three" motif is handled with appropriate restraint; Mina notes it without the narrative over-explaining it. The new message about the car is a good escalation. The stranger at the gates asking about Imogen is a solid complication. The chapter plan called for a clue suggesting accident rather than threat, and the car detail delivers that. WEAKNESSES: The opening paragraph — "By lunch, the story had already begun to rot" — is a bit too pleased with itself. "Not the real story. That was still hidden away in whatever unpleasant corner of the town it lived in" is slightly overwrought for the voice being established. The school gossip montage that follows ("Apparently there was a man... Apparently there had been a body") is a functional device but feels slightly mechanical — a list of rumours that doesn't quite earn its place beyond establishing chaos. It's competent but not inspired. The memory fragment near the end — "the reservoir, the shouting, the crash of water against the bank below" — is handled reasonably well, but the line "There was a difference between not knowing and being made not to know" is a bit too neat, too aphoristic. It's the kind of line that sounds profound but is actually just stating the obvious in a slightly elevated way. It's the chapter's most "tell-don't-show" moment. The photo detail (Eve having taken a picture from too close because she was angry and "wanted evidence of something, even if she hadn't known what") is a nice character touch that feels authentic. PACING: The chapter moves well. Scenes are short and propulsive as intended. The bell ringing at the end to push Eve into action is a bit of a cliché, but it's used lightly enough not to grate. DARK COMEDY BALANCE: The lightness and darkness are well-balanced. The humour never undercuts the genuine unease, and the unease never becomes melodramatic. This is the chapter's most consistent achievement. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED METAPHOR: Mostly avoided. The "rot" metaphor in the opening is slightly strained. "The room seemed to tighten around that sentence" is a mild cliché. Otherwise the prose stays clean and functional. OVERALL: A confident, well-executed chapter that delivers on its plan, maintains voice, advances plot, and handles its characters with care. Its weaknesses are minor — a slightly self-congratulatory opening, one too-neat aphorism, and a rumour-list that's functional rather than inspired. It doesn't reach masterpiece level, but it's genuinely good YA fiction with a strong sense of place and character. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 4 By the time school emptied out, the rain had come properly, the kind that made the whole town look temporarily disappointed in itself. Eve waited ten minutes after the last bell, which was mostly so she could convince herself that sneaking off was a choice and not a panic response. Mina had tried to come with her, but Eve had said no in the tone of someone trying to sound brave and mostly succeeding in sounding obstinate. “You’re making a terrible decision alone,” Mina had told her. “I know.” “That’s not reassuring.” “Wasn’t meant to be.” Now she was walking fast along the path behind the leisure centre, hood up, phone in one hand, keys digging into her pocket like a reminder that she was still, technically, a person with responsibilities. She’d texted her mum a lie about staying behind for a revision thing, which was stupid because her mother would know it was a lie within about four minutes, but by then Eve intended to be somewhere else. The location pin from the message had led her here, to the old track beside the reservoir and the strip of scrubby grass that separated the road from the water. It was the sort of place people used for dog walks, illicit cigarettes, and crying in private if they didn’t mind getting cold. She hated it immediately. The reservoir itself was hidden behind a low fence and a row of stubborn trees, the water a flat grey sheet through the branches. A sign by the gate warned about drowning in the brisk, official tone of the council. As if being told off by laminated paper would stop anyone doing anything. Eve checked the message thread again. **Ask her about the car.** The text had been sitting there all afternoon like a splinter under the skin. She pulled her coat tighter and carried on down the track. The concrete was slick with rain. A bus hissed somewhere on the road above her. Somewhere behind her, a dog barked, angry and distant. At the bend in the track she stopped. There was a car parked half on the verge, dirty silver, engine off. A man was standing beside it under the open boot, one hand braced on the frame. He looked, in the weak light, exactly like Ben’s description: grey coat, damp hair, the general air of someone who had spent too much of his life waiting in car parks. Eve’s entire body tightened. He was holding something small in his hand, looking down at it with the kind of concentration that suggested it mattered very much. Then he noticed her. His face changed in one quick, unreadable motion. Eve took one involuntary step back. “Eve Mercer?” he called. His voice was careful. Not threatening. Which somehow made it worse. She did not answer. He shut the boot slowly and came a few paces away from the car, keeping his hands visible in the way people do when they want to seem harmless and are therefore immediately more suspicious. “I’m not here to cause trouble,” he said. “That’s what people say before they cause trouble.” He gave a faint, tired smile that didn’t quite become one. “Fair.” She could see now that he was younger than she’d expected, maybe early forties, with the strained look of someone who had not slept properly in a long time. There was a bruise-coloured shadow under one eye. His coat was wet through at the shoulders. “Who are you?” Eve asked. He hesitated. “Call me Martin.” “That doesn’t answer anything.” “No. It doesn’t.” Rain ticked against the bonnet of the car. Eve stood with her phone ready in her hand, thumb hovering over the emergency call button in a way that made her feel both ridiculous and less ridiculous than she wanted to be. Martin looked towards the reservoir, then back at her. “Did your mother tell you what happened?” he asked. “She told me not to make a thing of it.” A grim flicker moved over his face, almost pain. “That sounds like her.” Eve’s anger rose fast and hot. “Don’t talk about my mum like you know her.” “I do know her,” he said, then immediately seemed to regret how that sounded. “Not well. But enough.” She stared at him. He rubbed a hand over his mouth. “Listen. I shouldn’t have come to the school. I know that. I just—” He stopped, swallowed, and tried again. “I needed to know if you’d been told the truth.” “The truth about what?” He laughed once, without humour. “That’s the problem, isn’t it.” Eve took another step back. The fence rattled somewhere nearby in the wind. Martin glanced at her phone. “Your brother said you’d ask that.” “Did you speak to Ben?” “Only briefly.” “Why are you here?” He looked at her as if the answer was obvious and horrible and embarrassing all at once. “Because of the car,” he said. Eve’s throat tightened. That word again. The car. Not the reservoir. Not the shouting. The car. “What car?” she said. He closed his eyes for a second. Then, with visible reluctance, he said, “The one your father put in the water.” The world went oddly thin. Eve stared at him, trying to make the sentence arrange itself into something sensible. “What?” “The old road by the reservoir,” Martin said, voice low now. “Three years ago. There was an accident. Your father was driving. Your mother was in the passenger seat. Imogen was in the back.” Eve’s pulse was loud in her ears. “No,” she said. Martin looked at her with a kind of misery that made her stomach twist. “I’m sorry,” he said. “I really am. But there was a car. It went over the edge. It shouldn’t have been there, and it was there.” “That’s not—” Eve stopped. Started again. “That’s not what happened.” He didn’t answer, which was somehow worse. She was remembering now in ugly, misplaced fragments. Not a clean memory, never that. A torch beam in the dark. Wet hands. Someone shouting her name. Her mother saying, too sharply, “Get back.” The smell of mud and petrol and rain. A car horn maybe, or something like one, cut off fast. But none of it was arranged properly. It had always been a collage, and somebody else had been holding the scissors. “You’ve got the wrong person,” Eve said. “I don’t think I do.” “Why would my dad—” “Because he was scared,” Martin said, and then, immediately, “Because we all were.” His voice had changed. Less careful now. More tired and raw. Eve’s face burned. “Who is ‘we’?” Martin looked at her for a long moment, then reached into his coat pocket and took out a folded piece of paper. It was damp at the edges. He did not hand it to her straight away. “Your mother asked me not to tell you,” he said. The words hit harder than anything else had. Eve laughed once, sharply and without joy. “Oh, brilliant. Fantastic. So this is a family newsletter.” “I’m serious.” “So am I.” She snatched the paper from him. It was a printout of a message thread, rough and grainy, the top part cut off. A date. A time after midnight. And underneath, her own number saved as “Eve.” There was a line beneath it, only partly visible because the paper had folded. **If she asks, tell her it was the brake.** Eve looked up so fast her neck hurt. “What is that?” Martin’s mouth tightened. “Something your mother sent.” “No.” “Yes.” “No, she didn’t.” “She did.” He looked away. “And there’s more.” He reached into the boot of his car and pulled out a plastic evidence bag, the kind you’d expect to see in a police drama, only this one contained a cracked mobile phone with mud on the corner. Eve’s stomach dropped. “That was found near the track,” he said. “It belonged to Imogen.” The air seemed to go very still. Eve stared at the phone inside the bag. It felt impossible that a thing so ordinary could be the centre of everything. “She texted you,” Martin said quietly. “Or tried to. That’s the number that sent the blood drops.” Eve looked up at him. He nodded once, miserably. “She’s been trying to get you to see this for weeks.” A drop of rain slid from the hood of Eve’s coat to the tip of her nose. She did not wipe it away. “Why?” she whispered. Martin’s expression broke a little. “Because she was in the car,” he said. “And because your dad wasn’t the only one who lied.” Something inside Eve gave way with a small, awful click. Not the truth, not yet. Something before it. The idea that the thing she’d been circling all day wasn’t just a weird text or a prank or an old family embarrassment. It was a shape. A cover-up. A bruise pressed into the dark until it became visible. She looked back at the reservoir fence, the grey water beyond it, the council sign warning people not to die. Then she looked at Martin. “Where is Imogen now?” she asked. He didn’t answer at once. And in that pause, Eve knew. Not the whole truth. Just enough. “She’s alive,” Martin said at last. “But if your mother finds out you’re here, she’ll have a fit.” Eve gave a short, incredulous laugh. “Mate,” she said, voice shaking now despite her best efforts, “I think that might be the least of everyone’s problems.” Martin bowed his head as if he could not argue with that. Eve stared at the phone in the evidence bag, the broken screen reflecting a pale strip of sky, and felt the last of her ordinary Thursday fall away. Behind her, a car door slammed on the road above. Both of them looked up. And then, from somewhere near the path, a woman’s voice called her name in a way that made Eve’s skin go cold.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a strong execution of the plan's core requirement: a physical location, a discovery, and the revelation that the protagonist has been lied to by someone close. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Eve's voice is consistently maintained throughout. Her defensive humour ("That's what people say before they cause trouble"), her physical self-awareness (thumb hovering over emergency call), and her sharp, brittle responses to Martin all feel true to the character profile. The line "Mate, I think that might be the least of everyone's problems" lands well as a moment of voice reasserting itself under pressure. Martin is rendered with appropriate ambiguity — tired, guilty, not villainous — which matches the story's stated intention to avoid melodrama. The chapter avoids making him a thriller-movie antagonist. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely strong. The exchange about Mina trying to come along is economical and characterful. The Martin-Eve confrontation has good rhythm, with Eve's resistance feeling earned rather than performative. "That sounds like her" / "Don't talk about my mum like you know her" is a particularly effective small exchange. Occasionally the dialogue tips into slightly expository territory — Martin's revelation of the car going into the water is delivered quite baldly — but this is partially justified by the scene's tension and Martin's exhausted directness. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter follows its plan closely. The protagonist goes somewhere she shouldn't, discovers a physical trace (the cracked phone in the evidence bag), and ends with the realisation she's been lied to. The plan called for "a photo, receipt, blood-stained item, note, or old phone" — the old phone is used. The chapter ending with a voice calling Eve's name is a strong pressure-based cliffhanger consistent with the plan's instruction to end on "emotional or social pressure." The revelation about the car going into the reservoir is well-timed and genuinely unsettling. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter earns its emotional weight. The moment where Eve's fragmented memory surfaces — "a torch beam in the dark, wet hands, someone shouting her name" — is handled with restraint and is more effective for it. The "small, awful click" metaphor for something giving way internally is one of the chapter's best moments. The council sign joke ("warning people not to die") is a good example of the darkly comic tone being maintained without undercutting the tension. PROSE QUALITY: The prose is clean and controlled. The opening description of rain making "the whole town look temporarily disappointed in itself" is a good, grounded image that fits the setting and tone. There are a few moments where the writing reaches slightly too hard — "the last of her ordinary Thursday fall away" is a touch overwrought — but these are minor. The chapter largely avoids purple prose and forced metaphor. The physical details (rain ticking against the bonnet, the council sign, wet shoulders) are well-chosen and specific without being showy. WEAKNESSES: The chapter's main weakness is that Martin's exposition is somewhat front-loaded. He delivers quite a lot of information in a short space, and while the scene's tension helps carry it, there's a slight sense of the plot being moved forward by a character who exists primarily to deliver information. The evidence bag feels slightly procedural — it's a convenient physical object that confirms everything — though the cracked phone is a good specific detail. The chapter could also be criticised for moving slightly too quickly from Eve's denial to her acceptance of the basic facts; her resistance collapses fairly rapidly once Martin starts talking. COHERENCE: The chapter is internally coherent and consistent with what has been established. The references to Ben, Imogen, and the family history all track with the character profiles and earlier chapter plans. CREATIVITY: The chapter avoids the most obvious thriller beats. Martin is not threatening, the location is mundane rather than gothic, and the revelation is human rather than sensational. The use of the evidence bag is slightly genre-conventional, but the overall scene avoids cliché. OVERALL: A well-executed chapter that delivers on its plan, maintains voice, and earns its emotional moments without overreaching. Minor weaknesses in exposition delivery and one or two slightly strained phrases, but these don't significantly undermine the chapter's effectiveness. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 5 “Eve.” Her mother’s voice, sharp with panic and anger and the particular kind of fear that arrives after you’ve already made the wrong choice. Eve turned. Tamsin was striding down the track in trainers and a wet coat, hair coming loose from its clip, face pale under the rain. Behind her, half visible through the trees, was Gareth’s car. Of course he was here too. Of course he was. “This is ridiculous,” Tamsin said, and then, to Martin, in a tone so clipped it could cut paper: “What do you think you’re doing?” Martin lifted both hands. “I didn’t mean—” “You absolutely did,” she snapped. Eve looked from one to the other. “You know him.” Tamsin shut her eyes for one second, as if the whole scene might disappear if she concentrated hard enough. “Eve, get in the car.” “No.” Her mother’s expression tightened. “Don’t do this now.” “I’m not doing anything. That’s the point. Nobody is telling me anything.” Gareth had come closer now, rain darkening the shoulders of his coat. He looked as though he had been dragged out of some other, easier life and was furious to find this one waiting for him. “Can we not do this here?” he said. Eve let out a laugh, sharp and disbelieving. “Oh, that’s funny. Because that’s exactly what you’ve all been doing. Here. Everywhere. For three years.” No one answered. The silence was answer enough. Martin looked at Tamsin, almost apologetic. Tamsin looked at the ground. Gareth looked offended that Eve had a right to be angry. It was a deeply familiar family tableau, only now there was a reservoir behind them and a broken phone and nobody could pretend it was normal. Eve took one step back and nearly slipped on the wet path. “Right,” she said, voice suddenly thin. “Brilliant. So someone wants to explain the car?” Tamsin’s face changed. Not much. Just enough. Eve saw it and felt a sick jolt of vindication and dread. “There was an accident,” she said. “That’s not what Martin said.” Tamsin glanced at Martin as if he’d betrayed her personally. Gareth’s jaw tightened. “Martin shouldn’t have involved you.” “Oh, good,” Eve said. “Excellent. I’m glad to know even my own life is in the ‘shouldn’t have involved you’ category.” “Eve—” “No. Don’t ‘Eve’ me.” She pointed at the evidence bag in Martin’s hand. “That’s Imogen’s phone, isn’t it? Why does he have it? Why is she missing? And why did Mum tell me not to make a thing of it if someone nearly drowned three years ago?” That made Tamsin go still. Too still. Rain ran down her face and she did not wipe it away. “It wasn’t like that,” she said. Eve stared at her. “Then what was it like?” No one spoke. Then Gareth said, too quickly, “There was a crash.” “Into the reservoir,” Eve said. He didn’t answer. That was worse than yes. “I remember the shouting,” Eve said, because once she started, the words came out in ugly, disordered pieces. “I remember you being angry. I remember Mum saying don’t tell her. I remember—” She stopped, because the next bit felt impossible. “I remember somebody crying in the car.” Tamsin flinched. “So what?” Eve said. “What actually happened?” Mina would have told her she was looking too much like a victim and not enough like someone about to start a row. But Eve was past tidy now. She was shaking with it. Gareth rubbed a hand over his face. “We hit the edge.” “With a car?” “Yes.” “Whose car?” His mouth flattened. “Mine.” Eve laughed again, but this one came out wrong, half hysterical and half furious. “Of course it was.” “We were arguing,” Tamsin said, very quietly. “I was driving.” Eve turned to her. “What?” Her mother looked as though each word might cost her. “We’d all been at the reservoir. I was driving us back. Gareth was… not in a good state.” “Are you serious?” “Eve, please.” “No, because this is mad.” She looked at Gareth. “You were in the car too?” He nodded once. “And Imogen?” Tamsin swallowed. “She was in the back.” Eve stared at them. The memory she had been carrying for years shifted shape with an awful, grinding slowness. The voice she thought had been Gareth’s. The hand on the door. The flash of headlights. The wet, horrible lurch as the tyres lost grip on the gravel by the track. “You let me think—” Eve began, then stopped because she didn’t know what she had let herself think. That it was just an argument. That it was about nothing. That the adults had handled it, as adults supposedly did. “That night,” Tamsin said, “we were all frightened. Imogen was hurt. Your dad panicked. I panicked. Everyone made bad decisions.” “Good,” Eve said. “Lovely. Great to know panic is hereditary.” Gareth flinched at that, which briefly pleased her. Martin shifted awkwardly beside the boot of his car, as if he understood exactly how little he ought to be taking up space in this catastrophe. Eve turned on him. “Why do you have her phone?” He looked down. “Because she gave it to me.” “Why?” “Because she wanted someone to keep it safe.” “Safe from what?” “From your father,” Gareth snapped. Eve whipped back around. “Oh, that’s convenient.” “It’s true.” “Is it?” Gareth went rigid. “You think I wanted any of this?” “You’re the one who keeps saying ‘not now’ to everything.” “That’s because there’s always more damage if we talk too soon.” “You mean if I know too much.” His face hardened. “Yes, maybe that too.” Tamsin made a sharp, miserable sound. “Gareth.” “What? She’s allowed to know now?” Eve stared at him. The rain was colder than she’d realised. Her socks were getting damp. Her hands had gone numb around the edge of her phone. “Tell me,” she said, very quietly, and because she had said it like that, they all went still, “what happened to Imogen.” Martin answered before either parent could. “She came out of the car when it rolled,” he said. “Not far. She wasn’t badly hurt. But she saw enough to know what was being hidden.” “Which was?” Eve asked. Martin looked at Tamsin. Tamsin shut her eyes. Gareth said, with the sour look of a man forced into something he hates, “There was someone else there.” Eve frowned. “Who?” No one replied. Then Tamsin said, in a voice so quiet Eve nearly missed it, “Your father hit the cyclist.” The words landed like a stone dropped in water. “What cyclist?” “The man by the road,” Gareth said. “He came off the path. I didn’t see him properly. We swerved.” Eve went cold all over. “No,” she said. Tamsin kept going, because once she’d started there was no good place to stop. “He survived. He wasn’t killed. But he was badly injured and frightened and there was blood everywhere and Gareth panicked and I—” “You what?” “I told him not to call anyone yet.” Eve stared at her. “No,” she said again, more sharply. “No. You’re not putting that on me like it’s normal.” “It wasn’t normal,” Tamsin said, and now her own voice was breaking. “None of it was normal.” “Then why lie?” “Because we thought they’d blame us.” “Because they would have,” Gareth said. “Because you’d been drinking,” Martin said suddenly. The silence after that was vicious. Eve turned slowly to look at him. Gareth’s mouth opened. Closed. “Oh my God,” Eve said. Tamsin pressed her fingers to her forehead. “Enough.” “No, not enough. Absolutely not enough.” Eve’s voice was rising now, the anger finally finding a shape to sit inside. “You drove drunk? You hit a cyclist? You lied about it? And I’m supposed to just—what? Live in the house with you and not know?” “We were protecting you,” Gareth said. Eve stared at him as if he’d spoken in another language. “Protecting me from what? The consequences?” From the road above, a car engine started. Someone else was arriving, or leaving, and for one insane second Eve wanted to run after it and climb into the first seat going. Instead she looked at Martin. “And Imogen?” she said. “What did she do?” Martin’s face was grey now. “She started filming,” he said. “She had her phone out. She filmed the road, the car, your dad, all of it. She was terrified. She wanted to hand it in.” Eve felt the floor of the world shift under her. “Then why didn’t she?” “Because your mother begged her not to,” Martin said. Tamsin shut her eyes. Eve looked at her. “Mum?” Tamsin’s face crumpled, just slightly, into something raw and exhausted. “She was sixteen,” she whispered. “I thought if it came out, it would ruin her.” Eve gave a short, incredulous laugh that sounded close to a sob. “So instead you ruined me?” “No,” Tamsin said quickly. “No, darling, I didn’t mean—” But Eve had already heard the truth underneath it: not me. Not then. Not that way. She had simply been collateral. Her phone buzzed in her hand. She looked down. Unknown number. A final text. **Now you know. She wants you to meet her.** Below it, an address appeared. Not the reservoir. The hospital. Eve stared at it for a second too long. Then she looked up, rain on her face, and saw all three of them watching her with the same horrible, shared knowledge that things had finally broken open. And for the first time since midnight, she knew exactly who “she” was.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a confrontation scene that delivers the story's central revelation: a drunk-driving accident, a cover-up, a cyclist who was hit and left, and a teenage witness (Imogen) who filmed it all. The chapter follows its plan closely and executes the emotional mechanics with considerable skill. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. It sounds genuinely British and teenage without being performatively so. Lines like "Oh, good. Excellent. I'm glad to know even my own life is in the 'shouldn't have involved you' category" and "Good. Lovely. Great to know panic is hereditary" are exactly the kind of defensive, sharp-tongued responses the character profile promised. The rhythm of the confrontation — short exchanges, silences that carry weight, interruptions — feels authentic rather than theatrical. The revelation is handled with real craft. Rather than dumping everything at once, the truth comes out in layers: the accident, then the car, then the drunk driving, then the cyclist. Each beat lands before the next arrives. The pacing is controlled and the escalation feels earned. The moment when Martin says "Because your father hit the cyclist" and the silence after is genuinely effective — the chapter earns that beat. The emotional dynamics between Eve and her parents are well-rendered. Gareth's self-justifying defensiveness ("I was trying to protect you") and Tamsin's exhausted guilt feel true to their profiles. The line "Not me. Not then. Not that way. She had simply been collateral" is the chapter's emotional peak and it lands cleanly without being overwrought. The chapter also handles the "wrong conclusions" element of its plan well. Eve doesn't confront the wrong person exactly, but she does have to revise her understanding of who is responsible for what, and the emotional reversal — realising her mother begged Imogen to stay silent — is more painful than any simple villain reveal would be. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is almost entirely dialogue and reaction, with very little physical grounding. The plan called for a "recognisably British setting" with sensory detail — wet shoes, bad lighting, ordinary texture. There are gestures toward this (rain, wet coat, damp socks) but they feel thin. The reservoir setting is barely present as a place; it's more of a backdrop than an environment. The plan's emphasis on "short, propulsive scenes" with "strong sense of place" is only partially fulfilled. Martin is a functional plot device here — he delivers key information at convenient moments — but he's not a fully realised character. His role as exposition-deliverer is a little too neat. He answers questions at exactly the right moments, which slightly undermines the messiness the plan promised. The ending — the text arriving with the hospital address — is a bit convenient. The chapter plan called for an "emotional reversal" where "the person they blamed is not the real problem, but they are hiding something too." This is partially achieved, but the final text feels like a mechanical plot gear turning rather than an organic emotional beat.
